steps.title: Use the clam evidence
steps.content: The passage says archaeological evidence showed significant increases in clam size and abundance at the same time the Kwakwaka'wakw terraced garden method was used. A supported conclusion must stay tied to that evidence: the method affected the clam food source. It should not add claims about other groups, present-day use, or adoption by outsiders.

steps.title: Reject unsupported claims
steps.content:
• Choice B says non-Indigenous people adopted the practice after observing it, but the passage never discusses adoption by non-Indigenous people.
  • Choice C compares archaeological support for Kwakwaka'wakw songs with support for Temiar and Lakota songs, but the passage does not make that comparison.
  • Choice D claims contemporary Kwakwaka'wakw people no longer use those methods, but the passage gives no information about current use.

steps.title: Connect size and abundance
steps.content: Choice A says the ancestral practice maintained a food source and promoted its robustness. That matches the evidence: if clams became larger and more abundant while the terraced gardens were used, the practice both supported the food source and made it stronger. The answer is A.
